explicit_text: Pearl Harbor was a turning point for me and my family. After the attack in December 1941, my father was kicked out of his job at the Western Pacific Railroad in January, labeled a security risk. The Army pulled all Nisei off the West Coast, including me, and transferred us inland. It was a difficult time, as we were Americans but faced discrimination and suspicion following Pearl Harbor.
implicit_text: That surprise attack in December 1941 was a turning point for me and my family. After it happened, my father was kicked out of his job at the Western Pacific Railroad in January, labeled a security risk. The Army pulled all Nisei off the West Coast, including me, and transferred us inland. It was a difficult time, as we were Americans but faced discrimination and suspicion following that event.

explicit_text: My father worked as a section foreman for the Western Pacific Railroad, and he loved his job dearly. But in January after Pearl Harbor, the railroad took his job away, claiming he was a security risk. They ordered my parents off railroad property, which was devastating since the railroad practically owned Gerlach. This forced my family into very harsh living conditions in the desert.
implicit_text: My father worked as a section foreman for the railroad, and he loved his job dearly. They ordered my parents off railroad property, which was devastating since the railroad practically owned Gerlach. This forced my family into very harsh living conditions in the desert.

explicit_text: Gerlach was where my family lived before and after the war started. After my dad lost his job at the railroad, we had to move off railroad property into a small trailer out in the desert near Gerlach. It was a tough winter with no facilities, and my sister had to come from Tennessee to help care for my younger siblings. Gerlach was home, but the war changed everything for us there.

explicit_text: I served in the United States Army during World War II as a Nisei soldier. Despite being American, we faced discrimination and were initially given menial jobs like emptying garbage cans at Camp Wolters. Eventually, after the 100th Infantry Battalion proved themselves, I was able to train as a medic and later attend the Military Intelligence Language School. The Army was both a place of struggle and pride for me.

The military was both a place of struggle and pride for me.

explicit_text: I was assigned to the 1851st Service Unit at Camp Wolters, Texas, where I trained as a medic. Unfortunately, the unit was relegated to menial tasks for a long time, and I was denied promotion despite my efforts. It was frustrating to serve my country yet be treated as less than other soldiers. Still, I stayed committed to my role and my fellow Nisei soldiers.

explicit_text: The 100th Infantry Battalion from Hawaii made a great impression in Europe, which helped change attitudes toward Nisei soldiers like me. Their success opened doors for us to get better assignments and recognition. Before that, we were stuck with menial tasks, but their bravery helped improve our standing in the Army.

explicit_text: I spent six intense months at Camp Barkley, Texas, serving as a male nurse. The tour was nonstop with no time off, but it was an important part of my Army service. After that, I was sent to the Military Intelligence Language School to learn Japanese, which was a challenge since English was my first language.

explicit_text: Okinawa was the site of a tragic accident that affected my unit. My sergeant Nakajima and twelve interrogators died when their C-47 crashed into a peak in the fog. I was in the hospital with an infection at the time and missed going with them. It felt like fate kept me alive while my friends were lost there.

explicit_text: I flew reconnaissance missions in an F-10, a version of the B-25 Mitchell, during the war. One memorable incident was when my plane's life raft popped out over Fort Myers, Florida, causing severe vibrations and forcing us to bail out. Despite the crash, we all survived, and I even joked with my wife about being late for dinner after escaping the 'Quivering Queen.'

explicit_text: Near Fort Myers, Florida, I experienced a frightening incident when my B-25's life raft accidentally deployed mid-flight. The plane shook violently, and we had to bail out at 12,000 feet. Fortunately, everyone landed safely, and I was picked up near what is now Busch Gardens Tampa Bay.
